symbols.name: RunBackgroundTaskSupervisor
symbols.docs:
The per-run task registry — the registry IS the open set the park/exit
gates read. On `done` resolving, the supervisor does exactly one thing:
invoke `onCompletion` once, awaited and bounded (or remove immediately
for `silent` tasks); a task is removed the moment its completion
handling finishes, and every removal wakes a parked loop. The gate
methods (`isEmpty`/`count`/`changeCount`/`waitForChange`/`disposeAll`)
are engine-facing internals, deliberately absent from the public
`BackgroundTaskSupervisor` interface.

symbols.name: withTimeout
symbols.signature: async function withTimeout(work: Promise<void>, ms: number): Promise<void>

symbols.name: BackgroundTaskOutcome
symbols.docs: How one background task ended; `outcome` is the task's one-line result.

symbols.name: BackgroundTaskCompletionContext
symbols.docs: What a completion handler receives alongside the outcome.

symbols.name: BackgroundTask
symbols.docs:
The capability record a spawn site hands over. Every task declares how
its completion is handled — the type forces the choice; there is no
implicit default. Any task the model is expected to await must publish
in its `onCompletion` (the model has no other way to observe it);
`silent: true` is strictly for fire-and-forget work.

symbols.name: BackgroundTaskRow
symbols.docs:
One `list()` row. No status field: a listed task is running (or briefly
settling while its completion handler runs); completed tasks are removed
— history lives in the event stream.

symbols.name: BackgroundTaskSupervisor
symbols.docs: The run-scoped capability surface — exactly register / list / cancel.

symbols.name: BackgroundTaskLifecycleEvent
symbols.docs:
Task lifecycle facts for the run's event stream and records; the engine
stamps `seq`. Fields mirror the public camelCase task vocabulary.

symbols.docs: Human-readable one-line summary of a Zod validation failure.

symbols.name: TurnFacts
symbols.docs:
Loop facts handed to `turnBoundary` hooks after each committed assistant
turn; in-process camelCase. Two axes: the SHAPE of the turn that just
committed (`toolCalls`, `backgroundTaskCount`, `hasPendingSteers`) and
the run's BUDGET position (`turn`, `maxTurns`).

symbols.name: TaskRegistryGate
symbols.docs:
The loop's view of the run's task registry — the same registry the
public supervisor mutates; the gates are SDK-internal mechanism, so
these members are deliberately absent from the public interface.

symbols.name: TerminationMode
symbols.docs: How the run completes; fixed at assembly from `agentOutcomeFn` presence.

variants.name: { /** Reads the submission the terminal tool accepted in this batch. */ kind:...

symbols.name: AgentLoopContext
symbols.docs: Everything one run's loop needs; assembled by the runtime.

symbols.name: runAgentLoop
symbols.signature: export async function runAgentLoop<T>(ctx: AgentLoopContext<T>): Promise<void>
symbols.docs:
The loop spine — control flow only; streaming, records, and tool
dispatch live in their own modules. Never throws: every exit classifies
into exactly one `finish`, committed in the same synchronous block as
its decision so a late steer is never accepted-but-dropped.

One predicate gates both exits: `calls == 0 ∧ no pending steers ∧ task
registry empty ∧ inbox drained`. In text mode a bare-text turn finishes
the run when the gate is open; in terminal-tool mode only an accepted
submission does (the terminal binding evaluates the same gate at
submission time). `calls == 0 ∧ no steers ∧ inbox drained ∧ registry
non-empty` parks the run; wake sources are any inbox publish, any task
removal, any steer, and interrupt. A wake whose drain yields nothing
re-evaluates the gate: text mode completes with the existing final
text, terminal mode re-prompts the model (`maxTurns` backstops spin).

symbols.name: waitForWake
symbols.signature: async function waitForWake<T>( ctx: AgentLoopContext<T>, tasksSince: number, ): Promise<void>
symbols.docs:
Park until any wake source fires: a steer, an inbox publish, a task
removal, or abort. The race loser would otherwise stay registered until
an unrelated wake, so a race-scoped signal unhooks it as soon as the
winner settles.

symbols.name: normalizeBatch
symbols.signature: function normalizeBatch( calls: ToolUseBlock[], results: ToolCallResult[], ): ToolCallResult[]
symbols.docs:
The engine-owned invariant: every `tool_use_id` is answered, in
`tool_use` order, regardless of executor behavior. A missing result
becomes a synthetic error; no event is emitted for it.

symbols.name: projectToolResult
symbols.docs: The ONE serialization point: non-string content is stringified here.

symbols.signature: function classifyLoopError( error: unknown, signal: AbortSignal, ):

symbols.docs: Read through a call so control-flow narrowing never caches `aborted`.

symbols.name: ToolResultBlock
symbols.docs: A `tool_result` content block, the unit `appendToolResults` wraps.

symbols.name: PartialReason
symbols.docs: Why a salvaged partial assistant message never completed.

symbols.name: UserMessageOrigin
symbols.docs: Where one appended user message came from.

symbols.name: ConversationRecord
symbols.docs:
One line of the `messages.jsonl` conversation artifact, fed to the
records sink as it is appended. `assistant_partial` is salvage only —
it never reaches `llmMessages()`.

symbols.name: Conversation
symbols.docs:
The single provider-history list plus the records sink. Every append
writes both in one call (single-writer rule); `llmMessages()` is the
only history source for a provider request, and the read-only snapshot
handed to every tool batch.

symbols.name: AgentEventBody
symbols.docs:
The run event union before the handle stamps `seq`: run lifecycle,
tool execution, background-task lifecycle, and the provider stream
events forwarded unchanged. Older members keep their snake_case payload
fields; the task events mirror the public camelCase task vocabulary.

symbols.name: AgentEvent
symbols.docs: Every event carries `seq`; records serve resume, the stream is live-only.

symbols.name: AgentRunError
symbols.docs: Why a run failed, typed so callers never parse prose.

symbols.name: AgentOutcome
symbols.docs:
The terminal state of one run: the terminal tool's accepted submission
(or the final text in text mode), a failure, or a cancellation — always
with the run's summed usage and turn count.

symbols.name: AgentRunHandle
symbols.docs:
The public surface of one live run — the only capability for it; hosts
that need by-id access keep their own map.

symbols.name: EventStream
symbols.docs:
A push-fed queue consumed as one pull-based `AsyncIterable`:

- single consumer: a second `[Symbol.asyncIterator]()` call throws,
- pushes never block the loop; the buffer is unbounded (the consumer is
  in-process; fan-out and backpressure are host concerns),
- `close()` completes iteration once the buffer drains; later pushes
  are dropped (run-end disposal settles tasks after `run_finished`),
- an early `break`/`return()` detaches: later pushes are discarded while
  the run continues; a stream nobody iterates retains every event.

symbols.name: RunHandle
symbols.signature: export class RunHandle<T = string> implements AgentRunHandle<T>
symbols.docs:
Run-handle internals shared with the loop: one seq-stamping emitter, one
event stream, one abort signal, one steer queue drained at boundaries,
a finishing latch (flipped while a submission commits), and one atomic
finish.

symbols.name: ToolUseBlock
symbols.docs: A model-emitted `tool_use` block, the unit of batch dispatch.

symbols.name: ToolBatchContext
symbols.docs: Per-batch facts the loop hands the executor.

symbols.name: ToolExecutor
symbols.docs:
The engine's ONE piece of tool knowledge - an injected port. Registry,
concurrency cap, terminal ordering, hooks, and the per-call pipeline all
live behind it (in `src/tool`). The engine keeps only the invariant it
cannot delegate: after `executeBatch` returns, any unanswered
`tool_use_id` is filled with a synthetic error result so provider-history
validity never depends on executor correctness.

symbols.docs: Per-run provider-call configuration, fixed at run start.

symbols.name: CompletedTurn
symbols.docs: One successfully completed assistant turn.

symbols.name: addUsage
symbols.signature: export function addUsage( total: UsageSnapshot, turn: UsageSnapshot, ): UsageSnapshot
symbols.docs: Sum per-turn usage into a run total; cache fields appear once reported.

symbols.name: runAssistantTurn
symbols.signature: export async function runAssistantTurn( cfg: TurnConfig, conversation: Conversation, signal: AbortSignal, emit: (event: AgentEventBody) => void, ): Promise<CompletedTurn>
symbols.docs:
Stream one provider turn: forward each `LlmStreamEvent` as an event body
and return the completed message. When the turn dies instead (abort
mid-stream, `ProviderError`), accumulated text/reasoning is salvaged to
the records sink only — incomplete `tool_use` blocks are discarded
entirely — and the error is rethrown for the loop's catch-all to
classify by `signal.aborted`, never by error type.

no token material is logged and nothing is written. Refreshing tokens is
the Codex CLI's job.

symbols.name: AccessCredential
symbols.docs: How a credential rides the request: `x-api-key` or `Authorization: Bearer`.

symbols.name: Access
symbols.docs:
A credential scheme: where requests go (`baseUrl`) and as-whom
(`credential` plus per-attempt headers). An access scheme knows nothing
about wire protocols.

`headers()` is deliberately async and called once per attempt: it is the
seam a future token-exchange scheme (e.g. copilot) plugs into without
touching the client, wires, or retry gate. Static schemes return a
constant.

symbols.name: apiKeyAccess

symbols.docs: Static api-key access: first-party endpoints or any compatible base url.

symbols.name: codexAccessClaimsFromJwt
symbols.docs:
Claim parsing for a Codex access token: the payload is the second
`.`-separated segment, base64url without padding, json, carrying a
non-blank account id under the namespaced auth claim.

Failures throw `ProviderError` kind `request` with lowercase messages.
This function reads only the token value supplied by the caller; it
never loads a credential cache file.

symbols.name: codexCodingPlanAccess
symbols.signature: export function codexCodingPlanAccess( baseUrl: string, accessToken: SecretString, ): Access
symbols.docs:
Codex coding-plan access: ChatGPT-managed bearer plus account routing
headers derived from the token's JWT claims at construction time.

symbols.name: LlmClient
symbols.docs:
A provider-neutral streaming model client.

Leg contract:
- single-pass: the returned iterable may be iterated once,
- success: zero or more deltas, then exactly one
  `assistant_message_complete`, then end (an empty assistant message is
  legal; absent usage fields default to zero),
- a stream that ends without the provider terminal event throws a
  truncated-stream `ProviderError` of kind `decode`,
- all other failures throw `ProviderError` from leg - except
  cancellation: when `options.signal` aborts, the abort error is rethrown
  as-is and callers classify by `signal.aborted`, never by error type.

symbols.name: ProviderError

A normalized upstream provider failure. Callers branch on `kind`, never on
message text. `request_id` is the provider's opaque http
`request-id`/`x-request-id` header.

symbols.name: toProviderError
symbols.signature: export function toProviderError( error: unknown, phase: "open" | "stream", requestIdFallback?: string, ): ProviderError
symbols.docs:
Map an error thrown by an sdk call to the owned taxonomy. `phase` selects
the fallback for errors with no http status: request construction failures
at `open`, stream parse failures while iterating a `stream`. Abort errors
never reach this mapping - providers rethrow them as-is when the caller's
signal is aborted.

symbols.name: StopReason
symbols.docs:
A parsed provider stop reason. Known values are named; any other provider
string passes through verbatim (parse-don't-validate).

symbols.name: LlmStreamEvent
symbols.docs:
A single normalized event from a streaming model invocation.

The three delta variants are "visible output" for the retry gate;
`assistant_message_complete` is the success terminus and leg ends
after it.

symbols.name: createLlmClient
symbols.signature: export function createLlmClient( connection: ProviderConnection, options: ProviderClientOptions =
symbols.docs:
Construct an `LlmClient` for a named provider connection: resolve the
profile, bind the access scheme to the wire as its transport, and wrap the
composition in the generic stream client. Invalid connections and codex
JWT-claim failures throw here (`ZodError` / `ProviderError` kind
`request`); the returned client honors the Phase 02 `LlmClient` leg
contract unchanged.

symbols.name: secretString

symbols.name: ResolvedProfile
symbols.docs: A profile resolved to its composition parts; consumed by the factory.

symbols.name: resolveProfile
symbols.docs:
The only vendor-aware mapping in the package: provider id -> { connection
schema (above), wire, wire options, access, default `base_url` }. Adding a
provider is one access module and/or one wire module plus one entry here.

symbols.name: isRetryable
symbols.signature: function isRetryable(cfg: RetryConfig, error: ProviderError): boolean
symbols.docs:
Retryable iff `rate_limit`/`server` with a status in `cfg.status_codes`, a
`transport` failure, or a truncated-stream `decode`. Parse-failure
`decode`, `authentication`, and `request` are never retried.

symbols.name: backoff
symbols.signature: async function backoff( cfg: RetryConfig, error: ProviderError, attempt: number, signal: AbortSignal | undefined, ): Promise<void>
symbols.docs:
Sleep `min(retry_after_s ?? base_delay_s * 2^attempt, max_delay_s)`. A
non-finite or non-positive delay skips the sleep. The sleep races the
abort signal: an interrupt during backoff must not wait out the delay.

symbols.name: retryStream
symbols.signature: export async function* retryStream( cfg: RetryConfig, attempt: () => AsyncIterable<LlmStreamEvent>, signal?: AbortSignal, ): AsyncGenerator<LlmStreamEvent, void, undefined>
symbols.docs:
Wrap a per-attempt stream factory in the visible-output retry gate.

One `emittedVisible` flag spans all attempts: once any delta variant has
been forwarded, a later failure fails fast - re-running the request would
duplicate text and double-dispatch tool_use ids. No new attempt starts
after `signal` aborts, and abort errors are rethrown as-is.

symbols.name: SecretString
symbols.docs:
A wrapper that keeps a credential out of logs, json, and inspect output.
`expose()` is called exactly once, inside a provider constructor.

symbols.name: withIdleGuard
symbols.signature: async function* withIdleGuard<T>( source: AsyncIterable<T>, idleTimeoutMs: number, abort: AbortController, ): AsyncGenerator<T>
symbols.docs:
Race each chunk against the idle watchdog: a stream that goes quiet is a
first-class `transport` failure, not a hang. On timeout the in-flight
request is aborted.

symbols.name: runAttempt
symbols.signature: async function* runAttempt( wire: Wire, request: LlmRequest, wireOptions: WireOptions, idleTimeoutMs: number, signal: AbortSignal | undefined, ): AsyncGenerator<LlmStreamEvent>
symbols.docs:
Run one wire attempt: open the sdk stream (the wire pulls per-attempt
headers from its transport), guard it with the idle watchdog, feed events
through the decoder, and enforce the leg contract (exactly one
terminal event; a clean end without it is a truncated stream). Caller
aborts are rethrown as-is.

symbols.name: LlmStreamClient
symbols.docs:
The one generic streaming client: a wire composed with an access scheme's
transport (bound in the factory), wrapped in the visible-output retry gate
and the idle guard. Implements the `LlmClient` leg contract
unchanged from Phase 02.

symbols.name: ReasoningEffort

Provider reasoning-effort hint. The neutral set is the union of both
providers' vocabularies; each encoder clamps to its provider's range.

symbols.name: UsageSnapshot
symbols.docs:
Token usage reported by a model provider. `input_tokens` counts uncached
prompt tokens; total prompt tokens are `input_tokens` plus cache read and
cache creation tokens. Absent input/output fields decode to zero.

symbols.docs: A neutral model invocation request.

symbols.name: AnthropicDecoder
symbols.docs:
The messages-stream state machine. `message_start` carries initial usage,
`message_delta` carries the stop reason and output/cache usage, tool-use
blocks assemble across `content_block_start`/`input_json_delta`/
`content_block_stop`, and `message_stop` is the terminus. Unknown event
and block types are ignored (forward compatibility; revisited when
server-side compaction blocks must be preserved).

symbols.name: encodeMessageItems
symbols.docs:
Project one neutral message onto responses input items: a `message` item
for its text parts (if any), plus `function_call`/`function_call_output`
items for tool calls and results. Reasoning is provider-managed and
dropped on encode (§5 caveat 1).

symbols.docs: Project a neutral request onto `POST /v1/responses` streaming params.

symbols.name: OpenAiDecoder
symbols.docs:
The responses-stream state machine. Text deltas stream from
`response.output_text.delta`, reasoning summaries from
`response.reasoning_summary_text.delta`, function calls assemble from
`response.output_item.added` plus argument deltas keyed by `item_id` and
close at `response.function_call_arguments.done`, and the terminus is the
completed (or incomplete) response snapshot. Unknown event types are
ignored (forward compatibility).

symbols.name: WireOptions
symbols.docs:
Vendor-specific encode quirks travel as data on the profile, never as
subclasses.

symbols.name: WireTransport
symbols.docs:
What a wire needs from its connection: where, as-whom, and per-attempt
extra headers. The credential shape structurally mirrors `AccessCredential`
so `wires/` stays import-free of `access/`; the two meet only in the
stream client and factory.

symbols.name: StreamDecoder
symbols.docs:
Per-wire decoder state machine: sdk stream events in, normalized events
out. Decoders accumulate per-block strings linearly and parse tool
arguments once at block close.

symbols.name: Wire
symbols.docs:
A protocol codec bound to one connection: encode the neutral request, open
one sdk streaming call per attempt, and construct the matching decoder. A
wire knows nothing about vendors or credential schemes.

symbols.name: WireFactory
symbols.docs: Binds a wire to one connection; the sdk client is constructed once here.

symbols.name: LlmClientContractBinding
symbols.docs:
One binding of the `LlmClient` leg contract (Phase 02 §4.5): the
scenarios decide how each behavior is induced (fixture-fetch client vs
live profile + eliciting prompt); the assertions are shared. Strictness is
data: deterministic bindings add the `exact` golden block, live bindings
omit it. There is no live/unit branching inside scenario bodies.

symbols.docs:
A fetch double that replays one response factory per call (the last factory
repeats) and records each request body for encode assertions.

symbols.docs:
A complete SSE response with the given body and headers. The recorded
fixtures end without the final blank-line frame terminator; a live
provider always terminates frames, so the missing terminator is restored
at the transport double.

symbols.docs: A JSON error response, the shape both providers use for http failures.

An SSE response that emits a prefix and then hangs open. The stream errors
when the request signal aborts, like a real socket teardown.

symbols.name: NotificationInbox
symbols.docs:
The system-side twin of the steer queue: a plain mailbox of host-published
strings, drained by the loop one priority below steers. Exhaustiveness
property hosts may rely on: every entry is a host publish — the SDK
injects nothing.

symbols.name: systemNotificationMessage
symbols.docs:
The one rendering helper: wrap a JSON payload as a user message holding
`<system_notification>{json}</system_notification>`. Rendering happens at
the loop's drain; the inbox stores plain strings.

Every `<` in the serialized payload is escaped to its unicode JSON
escape sequence (still valid JSON), so untrusted text (command output,
task summaries) can never spoof the tag boundary.

symbols.name: Notifier
symbols.docs:
The run-scoped publish capability (one per run, same instance on the
handle and on every tool-call context). Only the host publishes — from
tools, `BackgroundTask.onCompletion`, `turnBoundary` hooks, or the
handle; the SDK itself never does.

methods.name: publish
methods.signature: publish(message: string, opts?: { key?: string }): void;

symbols.name: LlmRef
symbols.docs: A named model profile; `AgentSpec.llm` resolves against the config keys.

symbols.name: LlmClientProfile
symbols.docs:
One model profile, as parsed objects — the SDK never reads a config
file. `connection` is the normal path (provider endpoint + credentials
as values); `client` is the injection seam for an already-built
`LlmClient` (tests, custom transports).

symbols.name: LlmClientConfig
symbols.docs: Provider credentials/model profiles, keyed by `LlmRef`.

symbols.name: buildLlmClientRegistry
symbols.docs:
Build every configured client eagerly: an invalid connection fails
`createAgentSdk` loudly, never a run.

symbols.name: JsonlRunRecorder
symbols.docs:
The per-run records writer: `<recordsDir>/<runId>/events.jsonl` (every
lifecycle event, engine `seq` preserved) and `messages.jsonl` (the
conversation artifact). Wired at construction, so records are lossless
from the first line regardless of when (or whether) anyone consumes
`events()`. Append-only and line-buffered: one ordered write queue, no
fsync guarantee — a crash can truncate at most the final line, and one
writer owns a run directory. A failed write latches and resurfaces on
`flush()`; it never disturbs the run.

symbols.name: AgentSdk
symbols.docs: The construction product: exactly one method.

symbols.name: AgentSpec
symbols.docs:
One agent template. `T` is the run's outcome payload type: the terminal
tool's accepted submission, or the final text (string) in text mode.

symbols.name: Agent
symbols.docs: Reusable template: any number of calls, concurrent runs allowed.

symbols.name: createAgentSdk
symbols.docs:
Bind the process-level configuration: build every llm client eagerly
(config errors fail loudly here, before any agent exists) and capture
the global hooks and records root.

symbols.name: startRun
symbols.docs:
Per-run assembly, in dependency order: records sink, inbox, notifier
(latched after finish), task supervisor, handle (the seq-stamping
emitter), conversation, the terminal gate, the bound executor, and the
detached loop. The same supervisor and notifier instances land on the
handle and on every tool-call context.

symbols.name: validateToolNames
symbols.signature: function validateToolNames( tools: readonly ToolDefinition[], terminalName: string | undefined, ): void
symbols.docs: Collisions silently shadow at dispatch, so they are construction faults.

symbols.name: TaskControls
symbols.docs:
A host task tool: registers a background task whose completion the test
controls. `silent: true` opts out of completion publication entirely.

symbols.name: scriptedTool

symbols.signature: export function writeTranscriptFixture( lines: JsonObject[], name = "transcript.jsonl", ): string
symbols.docs: Write an append-only JSONL fixture and return its path.

symbols.name: ToolResult
symbols.docs:
What a tool execution yields: an output the engine stringifies exactly
once, or a model-facing error. `metadata` is records/observability only.

symbols.name: ToolCallContext
symbols.docs:
What `execute()` receives: per-call facts plus the run-scoped
capabilities — the same supervisor and notifier instances the handle
exposes. `llmMessages` is a read-only snapshot taken at batch start: a
deliberate capability (transcript-aware tools); hosts that run
third-party tools should treat it as part of their trust decision.

symbols.name: ToolDefinition
symbols.docs:
The authoring surface — name, docstring, input contract, behavior.
There is no flag metadata: foreground / background / yield are runtime
patterns of `execute`, and the engine never branches on them. Build
instances through `defineTool`.

symbols.name: ToolDefinitionInit
symbols.docs: What a tool author writes. No behavior field, no flags.

symbols.name: defineTool
symbols.docs: The one construction site for `ToolDefinition`.

symbols.name: toolBatchExecutor
symbols.docs:
The `ToolExecutor` the engine injects. Ordinary calls run fully
concurrent under a cap of 8, results in `tool_use` order, thrown errors
and unknown names mapped to `is_error` results, abort settling with
straggler-emit suppression. Terminal calls execute AFTER every sibling
call has resolved (§4.1: the gate is evaluated at that point), in call
order; once one is accepted the rest of the batch's terminal calls are
denied by the gate's finishing latch.

symbols.name: executeCall
symbols.signature: async function executeCall( call: ToolUseBlock, byName: Map<string, BoundTool>, batch: ToolBatchContext, ): Promise<ToolCallResult>

symbols.name: projectContent
symbols.docs: String projection for events (results stay structured).

symbols.name: errorResult
symbols.signature: function errorResult(toolUseId: ToolUseId, content: string): ToolCallResult
symbols.docs: A settled error result with both clocks at the settling instant.

symbols.name: isAborted
symbols.docs: Read through a call so control-flow narrowing never caches `aborted`.

symbols.name: settledOrAborted
symbols.signature: async function settledOrAborted( lanes: Promise<unknown>, signal: AbortSignal, ): Promise<void>
symbols.docs: Resolve when every lane settles or the signal aborts, whichever first.

symbols.name: HookMatcher
symbols.docs: Exact tool name; absent matches all tools.

symbols.name: HookDecision
symbols.docs: Pre/post hooks speak only through this — one channel per signal.

symbols.name: ToolCallFacts
symbols.docs:
What pre/post hooks receive — per-call facts only, no conversation
access. A submission-vetting hook judges the payload alone; terminal
payload schemas must be self-contained enough to vet. This is a
designed constraint, not an omission.

symbols.name: HookEntry
symbols.docs:
The one extension engine: callbacks on three events, matched by tool
name. `preToolUse` deny means the call never executes; `postToolUse`
deny replaces the executed result; `turnBoundary` observes turn facts
and may publish through the provided notifier — it returns nothing.

variants.name: { event: "preToolUse"; matcher?: HookMatcher; run: (call: ToolCallFacts) => H...
{ event: "postToolUse"; matcher?: HookMatcher; run: ( call: ToolCallFacts, re...
{ event: "turnBoundary"; run: ( turn: TurnFacts, ctx: { notifier: Notifier; r...

symbols.name: HookEngine
symbols.docs:
Callback dispatch over one run's merged entries (globals first, then
per-agent). Pre/post entries matching the call run in parallel; any
deny wins and reasons join. A throwing pre/post hook resolves as deny
with the thrown message (fail-closed; a host wanting fail-open catches
inside its callback). A throwing turnBoundary hook is recorded and
skipped. A broken hook never wedges a batch.

methods.name: postToolUse
methods.signature: async postToolUse(call: ToolCallFacts, result: ToolResult): Promise<HookDecision>

symbols.name: matches
symbols.signature: function matches(matcher: HookMatcher | undefined, toolName: string): boolean

symbols.name: guarded
symbols.signature: async function guarded( run: () => HookDecision | Promise<HookDecision>, ): Promise<HookDecision>

symbols.name: SubmitCtx
symbols.docs: What the terminal handler receives alongside the payload.

symbols.name: SubmitVerdict
symbols.docs: The handler's verdict: commit-and-finish, or send the model back in-run.

symbols.name: AgentOutcomeBinding
symbols.docs: The unwrapped factory product; workspace-internal.

symbols.name: AgentOutcomeFn
symbols.docs:
How a terminal-tool run completes — opaque; minted only by
`createAgentOutcomeFn`. Its presence on an `AgentSpec` selects
terminal-tool termination; its absence selects text mode.

symbols.name: createAgentOutcomeFn
symbols.docs:
Mint the run's terminal contract: the factory owns the terminal tool's
identity (`name` is what the model calls and what hook matchers see;
`description` its docstring, derived from the schema when absent), the
payload schema, and the caller-owned submission handler. The default
`onSubmit` is the trivial validator — accept the payload unchanged.

symbols.name: unwrapAgentOutcomeFn
symbols.signature: export function unwrapAgentOutcomeFn<T>( fn: AgentOutcomeFn<T>, ): AgentOutcomeBinding<T>
symbols.docs: Workspace-internal accessor; the root package never re-exports it.

symbols.name: PipelineResult
symbols.docs:
Everything the pipeline owns about one settled call. The batch executor
constructs the `ToolCallResult` - it must pair the `tool_use_id`.

symbols.name: RunScope
symbols.docs: Run-scoped dependencies closed over when an executor is built.

symbols.name: BoundTool
symbols.docs: One definition bound through the pipeline; dispatched by the executor.

symbols.name: toolSpec
symbols.docs: Derive the wire declaration from the Zod input contract.

symbols.name: callFacts
symbols.signature: export function callFacts( runId: AgentRunId, call: ToolUseBlock, toolName: string, ): ToolCallFacts
symbols.docs: Frozen per-call facts shared by pre and post hooks.

symbols.name: rejectedResult
symbols.docs: A settled rejection with both clocks at the rejection instant.

symbols.name: bindTool
symbols.signature: export function bindTool(definition: ToolDefinition, scope: RunScope): BoundTool
symbols.docs:
Bind one definition to the per-call pipeline: abort check -> parse ->
preToolUse -> execute -> postToolUse -> stamping. Never throws; every
path returns a result the executor can record. Timing brackets
`execute()` only - pre-execution rejections stamp both times with the
rejection instant, and slow hooks never masquerade as slow tools.

symbols.name: stamp
symbols.signature: function stamp( result: ToolResult, startedAt: number, endedAt: number, ): PipelineResult
symbols.docs:
The pipeline's stamping for ordinary tools: errors are facts mapped by
the pipeline, never tool claims, and `is_terminal` is always false —
only the terminal binding stamps true.

symbols.name: TerminalGate
symbols.docs:
The submission gate the runtime closes over the run's live state. The
gate is SDK-internal mechanism, not a configurable hook — hosts tune
nothing here.

symbols.name: bindTerminalTool
symbols.signature: export function bindTerminalTool<T>( fn: AgentOutcomeBinding<T>, scope: RunScope, gate: TerminalGate, ): TerminalBinding<T>
symbols.docs:
Bind the minted terminal contract as an ordinary-looking tool whose
successful result ends the run. Order per call: schema -> gate -> pre
hooks -> post hooks (on the provisional result) -> `onSubmit`. Hooks
run BEFORE the handler so a hook deny — like a handler reject — costs
the host nothing; once `onSubmit` accepts, nothing can stop the finish,
so the accepted value and host state cannot diverge (single mutator).

symbols.name: buildToolExecutor
symbols.signature: export function buildToolExecutor<T>( input: BuildToolExecutorInput<T>, ): BuiltToolExecutor<T>
symbols.docs:
Bind the supplied definitions (plus the minted terminal tool, when the
run has one) through the pipeline and return the engine's executor over
a deterministically name-sorted registry (prompt-cache stability).
Name-collision validation happens earlier, at `createAgent`.
